query: evidence that supports using prescriptions as a proxy for diagnosing asthma in children

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Issues of Concern -- Asthma Action Plans
retrieved_docs.content: An asthma action plan (AAP) is a written document in which the family or caregiver is provided with up-to-date instructions regarding daily asthma medications, symptoms that show asthma control deterioration, response when these symptoms are identified, and steps in the case of an emergency. [54] [55] [56] The caregivers must be confident and careful when using the asthma action plan, as they must decide on initiating treatment at the onset of deterioration. A recent study indicated that a pictographic asthma action plan could be helpful for caregivers with low health literacy. [57]

retrieved_docs.title: Allergic and Environmentally Induced Asthma -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma is considered the most common chronic disease seen in children. It is a significant cause for missing school as well as frequent emergency department and urgent care visits. The use of inhaled corticosteroids has been shown to improve asthma control but adherence is low amongst all populations. In a study performed in Massachusettes between 2012 and 2015, the impact of the school nurse-supervised asthma therapy program on healthcare utilization was reviewed. The children who participated in this study were identified as high risk and had persistent asthma. The study recognized poor adherence to medications by this group of children as well. These children were given their inhaled corticosteroid monitored by the school nurse daily to twice daily. This was a retrospective study where data was analyzed and electronic health records were reviewed to follow asthma-related emergency department and hospital visits both before the study and after the study. Refills of albuterol and prednisone through pharmacy records and medical records were obtained. Lastly, the research assistants reviewed school absenteeism reports from school nurses.  [Level III Cohort Case-Control studies] The study showed a reduction in both emergency department and hospital visits as well as decreased refills in albuterol in the post-intervention group versus pre-intervention. This small study showed that collaboration with a school nurse to provide supervised asthma medication can reduce health care utilization in school-aged children and provide better asthma control overall. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Issues of Concern -- FeNO Testing and Pharmacotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: FeNO can be conducted in primary care settings and predict corticosteroid response. FeNO testing is not a replacement for standard assessment and should not be used in isolation to estimate disease activity and forecast future exacerbation. In addition, the costs associated with equipment and supply can restrict the utility of FeNO in the primary care office setting. Moreover, analysis requires experienced immunologists or pulmonologists to interpret the results. According to NHLBI guidelines, ICS should not be stopped only based on low FeNO levels. [6]

retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Interpret the signs and symptoms of asthma in the pediatric population. Identify medication classes that are used to treat children with asthma. Interpret how a stepwise treatment algorithm addresses asthma severity and control. Collaborate to provide interprofessional care for children with asthma to improve their quality of life. retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Treatment / Management -- Pharmacological Management
retrieved_docs.content: Asthma action plans are necessary for all patients with asthma. These plans are individualized and developed in collaboration with each patient and caregiver. They include detailed directions for managing asthma during periods of wellness, at the onset of symptoms, and during acute exacerbations requiring medical evaluation. [36] The NAEPP provides sample asthma action plans for children aged 0 to 5, patients aged 5 or older, and for use in school settings. Please refer to the Deterrence and Patient Education section of this activity for a link to a printable action plan. Clinicians develop an asthma action plan based on symptoms or peak flow readings and divide it into 3 zones—green, yellow, and red. Patients in the green zone are asymptomatic, with peak flows at 80% or better than their personal best. They feel good and continue to take their long-term control medication. Peak flow readings in the yellow zone are 50% to 79% of the patient's personal best, and symptoms such as cough, wheezing, and shortness of breath begin to interfere with activity levels. In the red zone, patients experience peak flow readings below 50% of their best, accompanied by severe shortness of breath, and an inability to perform everyday activities.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: In pediatric asthma management, patient education is pivotal in deterring exacerbations and promoting optimal disease control. Central to this approach is educating both caregivers and children about asthma triggers, recognizing symptoms, and the importance of following prescribed treatment plans. Caregivers and patients should understand how to recognize early warning signs of exacerbations and be instructed on when and how to seek prompt medical assistance. Furthermore,  teaching proper usage of inhalers, spacer devices, and monitoring techniques such as peak flow measurements builds confidence in managing asthma at home. In addition, it is essential to discuss the potential adverse effects of asthma medications with caregivers, empowering them to identify adverse reactions and make informed decisions about their child's healthcare.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Evaluation and Treatment of Asthma in Children -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the signs and symptoms indicative of asthma attacks in children. Assess pediatric patients in the prehospital setting using the Pediatric Assessment Triangle to determine the severity of asthma exacerbation and guide appropriate interventions rapidly. Apply evidence-based, individualized approaches for managing asthma attacks in the prehospital setting. Implement effective collaboration and communication among interprofessional team members to improve outcomes and treatment efficacy for pediatric patients experiencing an acute asthma exacerbation. retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Issues of Concern -- Compliance to Pharmacotherapy
retrieved_docs.content: Inadequate adherence in children with asthma is a leading cause of asthma attacks and a factor in asthma deaths. Subjective and objective measures for monitoring compliance have limitations; electronic monitoring devices (EMDs) can be considered. [51]

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Issues of Concern -- Inadequate Control
retrieved_docs.content: Increased use of albuterol (salbutamol) for >2 days a week for symptom relief typically indicates inadequate control and may require a step-up in treatment.

retrieved_docs.title: Asthma Medication in Children -- Function -- Essential Components of Management of Asthma in the Pediatric Population
retrieved_docs.content: Diagnosis of asthma Stepwise approach for management of asthma Emphasis on the use of preferred treatment at each step SMART (single maintenance and reliever therapy) before proceeding to a higher-step level of treatment. [26] Achieve adequate symptom control Management of asthma exacerbation Minimize adverse drug reactions Shared decision-making accounting for risks vs. benefits and personal preferences Patient education regarding inhaler techniques Improving patient adherence Assessment of environmental triggers Evaluation and management of comorbidities Overall reduction in mortality Assessment of quality of life by using a validated questionnaire (Pediatric Asthma Quality of Life Questionnaire) Consultation with a pulmonologist/immunologist if the patient requires care at step 3 or higher. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Evaluation and Treatment of Asthma in Children -- Evaluation -- Management Priorities Based on General Impression
retrieved_docs.content: In respiratory distress, priority interventions include positioning the child in a comfortable posture, administering supplemental oxygen or suctioning secretions as needed, and initiating targeted therapy based on the likely cause. For example, bronchodilators such as albuterol are administered for bronchospasm; diphenhydramine may be indicated for allergic reactions; and epinephrine may be used in cases of anaphylaxis. Further laboratory or radiographic evaluation may be considered based on clinical judgment and local protocols.

retrieved_docs.title: EMS Prehospital Evaluation and Treatment of Asthma in Children -- Evaluation -- Prehospital Provider Roles and Scope of Practice
retrieved_docs.content: The 2019 EMS Scope of Practice Model defines licensure levels and delineates provider roles to assist in legislative alignment across jurisdictions and promote consistency in EMS nationwide (see Image. Increasing Depth and Breadth of Knowledge from Emergency Medical Responder to Paramedic). [17] (Source: National Association of State EMS Officials , 2019)

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Asthma -- Consultations
retrieved_docs.content: According to the guidelines from the NAEPP and GINA, challenges in confirming an asthma diagnosis or uncertainties regarding a prior diagnosis warrant consideration for evaluation by a pulmonology or allergy specialist. This is particularly important if signs or symptoms suggest an alternative or exacerbating condition. Referral is also recommended for children with a history of severe asthma exacerbations, such as ICU admissions or requiring mechanical ventilation, as well as for those experiencing frequent hospitalizations or needing 2 or more courses of oral glucocorticoids within a year.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 113
retrieved_docs.content: Anti‑inflammatories, Inhaled Corticosteroids are classified as “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC,” linking this therapeutic group to the broader ATC category of inhaled agents used in obstructive airway conditions. Within this inhaled corticosteroid class, the specific agents beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate, beklomethason dipropionát (the same compound expressed in another language), and flutikason propionát (fluticasone propionate) are identified as child entities. The higher‑level category DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES is positioned as the parent class of “other drugs for obstructive airway diseases, inhalants in ATC” (inverse_isa) and is itself an instance (isa) of “OTHER DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES, INHALANTS in ATC.” This hierarchical structure clarifies that inhaled corticosteroid anti‑inflammatories are a subset of the inhalant drugs used to treat obstructive airway diseases such as asthma and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and it integrates multilingual drug names under a unified pharmacological framework for patient care and clinical decision‑making.

Non‑steroidal antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases are also “isa” members of the same ATC inhalant group, highlighting their role as steroid‑free anti‑allergic agents. The overarching category DRUGS FOR OBSTRUCTIVE AIRWAY DISEASES is an inverse‑isa of both adrenergics, inhalants and other inhaled drug classes, thereby encompassing all inhaled adrenergic agents, inhaled corticosteroids, antiallergics, and related combination products, while being inversely related to adrenergics for systemic use to distinguish inhaled from systemic formulations. These hierarchical and membership relationships provide a comprehensive framework for selecting appropriate bronchodilator, anti‑inflammatory, and combination therapies based on route of administration, receptor selectivity, and contraindications, supporting safe and effective patient care in asthma, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and related obstructive airway conditions.

Prednisone is listed both as an antiflogistika and as a child of this inhaled glucocorticoid group, indicating its use in inhaled formulations. Fixed‑dose combination inhalers pair a β‑agonist (albuterol, formoterol, vilanterol) with a corticosteroid (beclomethasone, budesonide, mometasone, fluticasone furoate) to achieve synergistic bronchodilation and anti‑inflammatory effects. Examples are budesonide/formoterol, mometasone/formoterol, albuterol‑beclomethasone, and vilanterol/fluticasone furoate. Recognizing the hierarchical relationships—parent‑child links, multi‑category memberships, and combination products—enables clinicians to select the most appropriate steroid formulation for upper‑airway (allergic rhinitis, nasal polyposis) and lower‑airway (asthma, COPD) inflammatory conditions, optimizing

The anti‑inflammatory inhaled corticosteroid group includes specific agents such as beclomethasone dipropionate monohydrate (also listed as beklomethason dipropionát) and flutikason propionát, each representing a child concept within the broader inhaled corticosteroid category. Overall, the therapeutic landscape for obstructive airway diseases integrates both adrenergic bronchodilators—delivered either by inhalation or systemically—and inhaled corticosteroid anti‑inflammatories. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 2 -- Community 1106
retrieved_docs.content: Antiallergic inhalants for obstructive airway diseases, excluding corticosteroids, are classified within the nedokromil category, and nedokromil itself is a descendant of the broader “N [Preparations]” group. Likewise, nusinersen is also a child of the “N [Preparations]” hierarchy, indicating that both nedokromil and nusinersen belong to a common parent class of preparations. In the realm of nasal therapeutics, “Other nasal preparations in ATC” are defined as a type of decongestants and other nasal preparations for topical use, situating them under the general class of NASAL PREPARATIONS. NASAL PREPARATIONS, in turn, encompass both topical decongestants (as indicated by the inverse‑isa relationship with decongestants and other nasal preparations for topical use) and nasal decongestants intended for systemic use (as shown by the inverse‑isa link with nasal decongestants for systemic use). Both mupirocin and nusinersen share the physiologic effect of increased protein synthesis, suggesting a common mechanistic outcome despite their different therapeutic classes. Additionally, “Other nasal preparations in ATC” are linked to mupirocin, reflecting that some nasal formulations may incorporate mupirocin or be categorized alongside it. Overall, the knowledge base connects antiallergic inhalants, nedokromil, and nusinersen through a shared parent class of preparations, integrates various nasal preparation categories under the umbrella of NASAL PREPARATIONS, and highlights the shared effect of increased protein synthesis for mupirocin and nusinersen, providing a cohesive view of how these drugs and formulations relate to each other in clinical practice.
